meta.title: AI-901 Practice
meta.description: Practice questions aligned with Azure AI Fundamentals (AI-901) study themes in this repo. Confirm objectives against the official study guide.

domains.label: Cross-cutting: NLP, vision, speech, generative AI

questions.question: Which is the most accurate description of generative AI?
options: Generative AI uses a language model to create original content in response to a prompt.
Generative AI is an older form of AI that has been superseded by machine learning.
Generative AI is so complex that only specialists such as data scientists can use it.
questions.explanation: Generative AI produces new content (text, images, code, etc.) from prompts, often using large language or multimodal models.

questions.question: What is an AI agent in the context of Microsoft Learn-style definitions?
options: A technology professional who builds AI applications.
Anyone who uses AI applications.
An AI application that can perform tasks on behalf of a user.
questions.explanation: An agent acts on behalf of the user to complete tasks, often with tools and orchestration—not merely a human role title.

questions.question: An AI application reads email aloud to a user. Which AI speech capability is being used?
options: Speech recognition
Speech synthesis
Sentiment analysis
questions.explanation: Text-to-speech (speech synthesis) converts written text into spoken audio. Speech recognition is speech-to-text.

questions.question: What is the purpose of tokenization in NLP?
options: To translate text into another language.
To summarize large documents.
To break down text into smaller units for analysis.
questions.explanation: Tokenization splits text into tokens (words, subwords, or characters) so models can process them.

questions.question: Which technique helps determine how important a word is in a specific document within a larger collection?
options: Naïve Bayes
TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency)
K-means clustering
questions.explanation: TF-IDF balances term frequency in a document with how common the term is across all documents.

questions.question: Which best describes the role of embedding vectors in NLP?
options: They duplicate tokens in multiple languages.
They define stopwords that should be ignored.
They capture semantic relationships between tokens in a high-dimensional space.
questions.explanation: Embeddings map tokens to vectors so semantically similar words are closer in vector space.

questions.question: What is a large language model (LLM)?
options: A type of AI model designed to generate human-like text.
A model that only processes images, not text.
A small, efficient model used only on mobile devices.
questions.explanation: LLMs are trained on large text corpora and are used for generation, completion, classification, and more.

questions.question: What does an attention layer do in a transformer model?
options: Removes irrelevant words from the input text.
Examines relationships between each token and other tokens in the sequence.
Flags inappropriate content in generated text.
questions.explanation: Self-attention lets the model weigh how each token relates to others when producing representations.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a system prompt?
options: To provide context and instructions to the AI model.
To configure a model to run on a particular operating system.
To store user preferences for future interactions only.
questions.explanation: System prompts set behavior, scope, and safety instructions separate from end-user messages.

questions.question: What are embeddings in generative AI contexts?
options: Extra words added by a transformer to enhance generation.
Small language models for niche tasks only.
Vector-based representations of tokens that capture semantic meaning.
questions.explanation: Embeddings represent meaning numerically; they underpin retrieval, similarity, and many NLP pipelines.

questions.question: A hiring model disadvantages applicants from certain regions because training data underrepresented them. Which Responsible AI principle is most directly violated?
options: Transparency
Fairness
Accountability
questions.explanation: Fairness addresses bias and discriminatory outcomes; mitigation includes better data and fairness testing.

questions.question: A warehouse robot uses computer vision and should avoid grasping when uncertain. Using probability or confidence scores before acting primarily supports which principle?
options: Inclusiveness
Reliability and safety
Privacy and security
questions.explanation: Probabilistic models can be wrong; systems should mitigate harm with thresholds, fallbacks, and monitoring.

questions.question: Temporary facial images at an access gate should be deleted when no longer needed. This practice aligns most closely with:
options: Transparency
Privacy and security
Reliability and safety only, with no privacy angle
questions.explanation: Minimizing retention of sensitive data supports privacy and security responsibilities.

questions.question: A voice assistant provides spoken responses but no captions. Users who are Deaf or hard of hearing cannot use it effectively. Adding captions best demonstrates:
options: Accountability
Inclusiveness
Fairness only, with no overlap
questions.explanation: Inclusiveness means designing so diverse users can succeed; multimodal output can remove barriers.

questions.question: A bank uses AI for loan decisions and explains to customers that AI is used and what kinds of factors inform the model (without leaking secrets). This supports:
options: Transparency
Tokenization
TF-IDF
questions.explanation: Transparency helps users understand automation, limitations, and how to contest or clarify outcomes.

questions.question: An organization documents governance, roles, and review processes for its AI systems. This primarily reflects:
options: Fairness
Accountability
Speech synthesis
questions.explanation: Accountability ensures humans and organizations own outcomes and have governance in place.

questions.question: You need to detect manufacturing defects from camera images on an assembly line. Which workload category fits best?
options: Natural language processing
Computer vision
Speech synthesis
questions.explanation: Image-based inspection is a classic computer vision scenario.

questions.question: You want to route customer emails to departments by intent (billing vs. technical). Which workload fits best?
options: Computer vision
Natural language processing
Robotics kinematics
questions.explanation: Text classification and understanding are core NLP tasks.

questions.question: The AI-901 exam emphasizes conceptual knowledge. Which study habit matches scenario-style questions best?
options: Memorize product names only.
For each service, know when it fits a problem—not only its definition.
Skip Responsible AI because it rarely appears.
questions.explanation: Microsoft’s guidance stresses scenario thinking: matching tools and principles to realistic situations.

questions.question: In a cloud context, an application that calls a managed AI service over HTTPS is primarily consuming:
options: On-premises tape backup
A service endpoint exposed by the cloud provider
A local-only SQLite file with no network
questions.explanation: Clients use endpoints (URLs), authentication, and request/response patterns to consume cloud AI services.

questions.question: In Microsoft’s learning path for AI-901, solutions often center on Microsoft Foundry for building and using AI. At a fundamentals level, Foundry helps teams:
options: Replace all databases with spreadsheets.
Develop and deploy AI applications and models in a governed workspace.
Eliminate the need for authentication in APIs.
questions.explanation: Foundry provides a platform for AI development, deployment, and tooling—confirm current names and features in Microsoft Learn.

questions.question: A client application needs to send prompts to a deployed language model. Which pair is most characteristic of production-style access patterns?
options: FTP and Telnet
REST/HTTPS API calls with authentication (for example keys or tokens) to an endpoint
USB bulk transfer only
questions.explanation: Apps typically call secured HTTP APIs; exact auth mechanisms depend on the service and configuration.

questions.question: Storing API keys in public source code repositories is problematic because:
options: It improves transparency for competitors.
It can expose secrets and allow unauthorized use of your resources.
It is required by Responsible AI.
questions.explanation: Secrets belong in secure configuration (vaults, environment variables, managed identity patterns)—not public repos.

questions.question: A single-agent solution in Foundry-style scenarios typically refers to:
options: Exactly one human reviewer worldwide.
An AI agent orchestration pattern with one primary agent (versus complex multi-agent topologies).
A deployment with no models.
questions.explanation: AI-901 skills reference single-agent solutions; study how Learn describes agents and orchestration in Foundry.

questions.question: Which architecture sketch best matches “client app → AI platform → model → response”?
options: User interface or backend sends requests to the AI service; the service returns model output to the app.
The model runs only inside the user’s browser cache with no server.
Training data is uploaded on every end-user keystroke without batching.
questions.explanation: Fundamentals exams expect you to trace request flow from app to service to model output.

questions.question: When reading a Python sample that calls an AI client SDK, you should be able to recognize that the client object usually:
options: Replaces the need for any authentication.
Encapsulates configuration and methods used to send requests to the service.
Only works with Java applications.
questions.explanation: AI-901 expects you to follow client setup, method calls, and response handling at a reading-comprehension level.

questions.question: Choosing a model for a task (chat completion vs. embeddings vs. vision) is primarily about:
options: Matching model capabilities to the input modality and desired output.
Always picking the largest model regardless of task.
Avoiding all Azure services.
questions.explanation: Different models support different modalities and tasks; selection should fit the scenario.

questions.question: Speech recognition (speech-to-text) converts:
options: Written text into audio
Spoken language into written text
Images into 3D models
questions.explanation: Recognition is speech→text; synthesis is text→speech.

questions.question: Text normalization in text-to-speech might convert “Dr.” to “Doctor” so the system:
options: Uses more disk space
Pronounces abbreviations naturally instead of saying individual letters
Skips audio generation
questions.explanation: Normalization expands abbreviations, numbers, and symbols into speakable forms.

questions.question: Object detection differs from image classification because detection typically:
options: Only outputs a single label for the entire image.
Locates objects (for example with bounding boxes) and may identify multiple objects.
Translates text in the image to speech only.
questions.explanation: Detection finds where objects are and what they are; classification assigns one label to the whole image.

questions.question: Human oversight for high-risk AI decisions (for example credit or health) is important because:
options: Models are probabilistic and can be wrong or biased.
Models are always 100% accurate.
Regulations never apply to AI.
questions.explanation: Responsible deployment often combines automation with review, escalation, and monitoring.

questions.question: At a high level, deploying a model makes it:
options: Available for applications to invoke through the platform’s interfaces.
Permanently impossible to update.
Only usable without any metrics or logging.
questions.explanation: Deployment exposes the model for inference behind endpoints and governance controls.

questions.question: Extracting entities (names, dates, amounts) from unstructured documents is primarily:
options: A pure audio task
An NLP / language understanding task
A GPU cooling task
questions.explanation: Entity extraction is a text understanding problem, often using NLP models.

questions.question: Explaining model behavior to users is encouraged, but sharing raw sensitive training records publicly would violate:
options: Transparency
Privacy and security
Inclusiveness
questions.explanation: Transparency does not require exposing private data; protect data while explaining at an appropriate level.

questions.question: Monitoring latency, errors, and usage for an AI endpoint helps teams:
options: Detect issues and improve reliability
Guarantee the model never updates
Remove all authentication
questions.explanation: Operational monitoring supports reliability and safety in production scenarios.

questions.question: Clear instructions, examples (few-shot), and specifying output format in a prompt mainly improve:
options: Predictability and usefulness of model outputs
Physical robot torque
Database normalization theory
questions.explanation: Prompt design steers the model toward the desired behavior and format.

questions.question: Testing AI systems for fairness across demographic groups is a response to risk that:
options: Training data and design choices can encode bias.
Cloud regions do not exist.
HTTPS is optional everywhere.
questions.explanation: Bias can enter through data, labels, and deployment context; testing and mitigation are key.

questions.question: Conceptually, why do applications use authentication when calling cloud AI APIs?
options: To identify and authorize the caller and protect resources
To disable encryption
To prevent logging
questions.explanation: APIs use keys, tokens, or managed identities so only permitted clients access paid or sensitive capabilities.

questions.question: A model that generates a summary of a support ticket is an example of:
options: Generative AI usage (creating new text)
Only hardware thermal management
Only rotating images by 90°
questions.explanation: Summarization produces new text conditioned on input; it is a common generative NLP task.

questions.question: Showing confidence scores or “I’m not sure” behavior for low-confidence predictions supports:
options: Reliability and safety
Irrelevant marketing
Deleting all logs
questions.explanation: Calibrated uncertainty and safe fallbacks reduce harmful automated actions.

questions.question: Placing API keys in environment variables or a secure vault instead of hardcoding aligns with:
options: Privacy and security practices for secrets
Avoiding all documentation
Disabling HTTPS
questions.explanation: Secret management protects against leakage and abuse.

questions.question: MFCCs are commonly used in speech recognition to:
options: Represent spectral features of audio frames in a compact form
Encrypt audio files
Replace the need for a microphone
questions.explanation: MFCCs capture perceptually relevant spectral information from framed audio.

questions.question: Documenting who owns model updates, incident response, and compliance reviews is part of:
options: Governance and accountability
Tokenization
Sampling rate selection
questions.explanation: Clear ownership and processes support accountable AI operations.

questions.question: In a typical app flow, the sequence “authenticate → send prompt → receive completion → render to user” emphasizes:
options: Secure, structured consumption of AI services
Storing model weights in the browser only
Running training on every click with no batching
questions.explanation: Production patterns combine auth, inference calls, and UI handling.

questions.question: A scenario needs both image understanding and text generation (describe a photo). This suggests:
options: A multimodal or vision-language style capability
Only a relational database index
Only TF-IDF without models
questions.explanation: Cross-modal tasks combine vision encoders with language decoders or multimodal models.

questions.question: What activity happens during the pre-processing stage of speech recognition?
options: The audio is converted to a proprietary video format only.
Background noise is intentionally added to the audio signal.
Feature vectors are extracted from the audio waveform for modeling.
questions.explanation: Pre-processing turns raw audio into compact features (for example spectral features) suitable for acoustic modeling.

questions.question: What are phonemes?
options: Artifacts removed from the signal only for music playback.
The smallest unit of sound in speech.
AI models that generate images from text.
questions.explanation: Phonemes are the minimal speech sounds used when mapping text to pronunciation in synthesis and recognition pipelines.

questions.question: Why is it important to generate prosody in speech synthesis?
options: Prosody maximizes the volume of the audio output only.
Prosody translates speech to the listener’s native language automatically.
Prosody helps ensure natural pronunciation, rhythm, and speech cadence.
questions.explanation: Prosody controls stress, intonation, and timing so synthesized speech sounds natural, not robotic.

questions.question: A checkout camera assigns one label to an entire photo of a single piece of fruit (apple vs. banana). Which task is this?
options: Semantic segmentation
Image classification
Object detection with multiple bounding boxes
questions.explanation: Image classification predicts a single label for the whole image based on its main subject.

questions.question: You need to find several products in one photo and know where each item appears. Which approach matches this goal?
options: Image classification only
Object detection (for example with bounding boxes)
Tokenization of the image file name
questions.explanation: Object detection locates multiple objects and their regions, not just one label for the entire image.

questions.question: Semantic segmentation differs from basic object detection because it typically:
options: Classifies each pixel by the object or class it belongs to
Outputs only a single label for the entire image
Converts speech to text
questions.explanation: Segmentation assigns a class per pixel, yielding precise masks of object regions.

questions.question: In a typical CNN for image classification, convolutional layers primarily:
options: Replace the need for any training data.
Apply filters to build feature maps that capture visual patterns before prediction.
Encrypt images for storage only.
questions.explanation: CNNs stack convolutions to extract hierarchical features (edges, textures, parts) summarized as feature maps.

questions.question: In a vision transformer (ViT), images are often processed by:
options: Splitting the image into patches and embedding each patch for attention-based modeling.
Ignoring spatial structure entirely without embeddings.
Using only TF-IDF on filenames.
questions.explanation: ViT models treat image patches like tokens, applying transformer attention over patch embeddings.

questions.question: When vision and language encoders are trained so images and text share a joint representation, models can:
options: Only classify images without any text output.
Relate visual features to language (for example captions or tags) in a shared embedding space.
Remove the need for any training data.
questions.explanation: Multimodal models align image and text embeddings, enabling captioning, tagging, and cross-modal search.

questions.question: Many modern image-generation models use diffusion: they start from noise and iteratively:
options: Remove noise and refine structure until the image matches the prompt-driven signal.
Increase noise until the image disappears.
Convert the image to MFCCs only.
questions.explanation: Diffusion models denoise step by step, conditioning on the prompt to synthesize a coherent image.

questions.question: Multimodal computer vision models that interpret relationships between objects and descriptive text are an example of:
options: Contextual or semantic image analysis beyond a single class label
Pure speech synthesis
Disk defragmentation
questions.explanation: Contextual analysis ties visual content to language for richer understanding (scenes, activities, descriptions).

questions.question: Digitizing sound for speech often uses thousands of samples per second (for example 16 kHz). For vision, an image is digitized as:
options: A grid of pixels with numeric color or intensity values per channel.
A single number with no spatial layout.
Only a text caption with no raster data.
questions.explanation: Images are discrete grids of pixels; many vision pipelines preprocess (resize, normalize) before model input.

questions.question: Computer vision typically refers to AI that:
options: Processes visual input such as images, video, or camera streams to extract meaning.
Only generates MIDI music files.
Only stores blobs in cold storage without analysis.
questions.explanation: Computer vision covers tasks like classification, detection, segmentation, and multimodal understanding of visual data.
